answer:
**In the Āṅgirasa Smṛti (Chapter 1), verses 219–223.**

A woman who has been married one, two, three, four, or five times, in very vile times that are full of sin—if she is discovered, one should question her well, investigate, and deliberate.[^1] [...] A *veśyā* (prostitute), though fit only for pleasure, is superior to her. If one eats in the same row with her during these rites, or eats together with her, it is a wicked act that causes loss of caste.[^2] For the purification of that sin, one should, according to rule, be covered in a pit in the earth, having dug it for a period of one watch (three hours) or two *ghaṭikās* (forty-eight minutes). After being lifted out of it, one should then perform the birth rites (*jātakarma*) and others anew. One should also perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances according to rule. With a controlled self, subsisting on barley, he should perform the Cāpāgrayāna rite; then he becomes pure. He should perform five thousand baths, either himself or through a Brahmin. Then he becomes pure for himself alone. He is not fit for others. Thus spoke Bhṛgu in ancient times.[^3]

[^1]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-55ed5b).

[^2]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-f9ae9b).

[^3]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-41d863).

args.query: buried pit watch ghaṭikā Taptakṛcchra Cāpāgrayāna
"one who eats" woman married buried pit
"Taptakṛcchra" pit OR buried OR ghaṭikā OR "one watch" OR "two ghaṭikās" OR Cāpāgrayāna OR Bhṛgu
"eats" OR "dines" OR "takes food" woman OR wife remarried OR "multiple times" OR punarbhū OR svairiṇī penance
"buried" pit OR "covered in a pit" OR "dug in the earth"
Bhṛgu OR Bhrgu smrti OR "according to Bhrgu"
